content_markdown:
The graph below shows the variation of the pressure $p$ with volume $V$ of an ideal gas during one cycle of an engine.

Which of the following correctly names the thermodynamic process associated with the parts $Y \rightarrow Z$ and $Z \rightarrow X$ of the cycle?

diagram_metadata.description: A pressure-volume (p-V) graph showing a triangular cycle with vertices X, Y, and Z. Process X to Y is a curve/diagonal decreasing in pressure and increasing in volume. Process Y to Z is a horizontal line moving to the left (decreasing volume at constant pressure). Process Z to X is a vertical line moving upwards (increasing pressure at constant volume).

options.label: A
options.text: Y $\rightarrow$ Z: isobaric; Z $\rightarrow$ X: isochoric

options.label: B
options.text: Y $\rightarrow$ Z: isobaric; Z $\rightarrow$ X: isothermal

options.label: C
options.text: Y $\rightarrow$ Z: isochoric; Z $\rightarrow$ X: isobaric

options.label: D
options.text: Y $\rightarrow$ Z: isochoric; Z $\rightarrow$ X: isothermal

vibe_explanation: To identify these processes, look at what remains constant on the p-V axes. For Y to Z, the line is horizontal, meaning the pressure $p$ does not change, which is an isobaric process. For Z to X, the line is vertical, meaning the volume $V$ does not change, which is an isochoric (or isovolumetric) process.
